danger_signals.meaning: Environmental hazard; aggressive creatures that attack on sight

danger_signals.signal: Boss enemies (Death Knight, Dr. Schabbs, Hans Grosse, Trans Grosse, Ubermutant)
danger_signals.meaning: Major threat; end-of-level confrontations requiring specific tactics
Active combat engagement; player is under fire

danger_signals.visual_form: Numeric HUD indicator, face graphic at 25% health shows blood
danger_signals.meaning: Proximity to death state; visual degradation as danger increases

safety_signals.meaning: Resource security; ability to sustain combat

safety_signals.visual_form: Concealed wall tiles that slide back when activated
safety_signals.meaning: Safe zones containing treasure, health, or ammunition
Restricted areas; protection from immediate threats

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Swastikas (removed in SNES censorship; replaced with multicolored stone walls and generic red sign text)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Nazi guards, Nazi soldiers, specialized units (Death Knight, Ubermutant), bosses with distinct appearances
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Castle Wolfenstein bunker architecture, Nazi-themed level designs, German military aesthetic

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Player character B.J. Blazkowicz (sprite with visible face in HUD)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Escape routes, elevators to next levels, safe zones implied through level progression

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: 2.5D sprite enemies positioned throughout level grid
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies engage player on sight; attack with firearms or melee depending on unit type

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Health percentage HUD display; face graphic degrades and shows blood at 25% health
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player movement speed may be affected; continued damage leads to level restart with minimal equipment

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Visible ammunition and health pickups scattered in levels
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must manage ammo usage; health recovery requires finding pickups

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Elevator graphics as level exit; boss encounter chambers
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must reach elevator or defeat boss to advance; completion displays ratio and speed metrics

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Episode naming and cutscene briefings (Japanese version uses black splash screens with episode names; US/EU uses text briefings)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player progresses through named episodes with thematic coherence

environmental_storytelling.example: Levels themed as Nazi bunkers and buildings establish setting and faction control of space
Keyed doors and wall barriers force player navigation and create sense of infiltration through secured facility
Guard positioning and density communicates security levels within different areas
Secret wall tiles that slide back when triggered suggest covert supply caches and hidden military installations
Gold and valuables scattered throughout Nazi facility implies looting of military/administrative assets
Elevator graphics serve as visual goal marker; reaching it represents successful escape or mission completion

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Patrol or stand in place within level grid spaces
Engage with gunfire; pursue using grid-based pathfinding
Significantly elevated health and damage output; occupies dedicated boss arena; requires sustained engagement
Normal movement speed and combat capability
Visual indicator (blood on face graphic) signals imminent death state; one additional hit triggers level restart

notes: Wolfenstein 3D SNES (1994) represents a heavily censored port of the DOS original. Nazi imagery, particularly swastikas, was removed and replaced with generic stone textures and red signs. Hitler (renamed 'Staatmeister') had his iconic mustache removed. Dogs were replaced with giant rats due to Nintendo policies. Despite these changes, the core visual communication system remains intact: grid-based level design, 2.5D sprite-based enemies, HUD-based health display, and elevator-based progression. The game communicates political situation (WWII Allied spy vs. Nazi forces) primarily through environmental architecture, enemy uniforms (though rendered minimally as 2D sprites), and mission context. Regional differences exist between US/EU and Japanese versions primarily in cutscene presentation (Japanese uses black splash screens vs. text briefings). The ending screen anomaly (boss order mismatch) suggests late developmental changes. No detailed color palettes or precise visual specifications are documented in available sources.
